## STABILIZED MEDICAL IMAGE ATTACKS

## **Gege Qi**[1] **, Lijun Gong**[1] _[∗]_ **, Yibing Song**[2] _[∗]_ **, Kai Ma**[1] **, Yefeng Zheng**[1]

1 Tencent Jarvis Lab, Shenzhen, China

2 Tencent AI Lab, Shenzhen, China

lijungong@tencent.com yibingsong.cv@gmail.com yefengzheng@tencent.com

## ABSTRACT

Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have advanced existing medical systems for automatic disease diagnosis. However, a threat to these systems arises that adversarial attacks make CNNs vulnerable. Inaccurate diagnosis results make a negative influence on human healthcare. There is a need to investigate potential adversarial attacks to robustify deep medical diagnosis systems. On the other side, there are several modalities of medical images (e.g., CT, fundus, and endoscopic image) of which each type is significantly different from others. It is more challenging to generate adversarial perturbations for different types of medical images. In this paper, we propose an image-based medical adversarial attack method to consistently produce adversarial perturbations on medical images. The objective function of our method consists of a loss deviation term and a loss stabilization term. The loss deviation term increases the divergence between the CNN prediction of an adversarial example and its ground truth label. Meanwhile, the loss stabilization term ensures similar CNN predictions of this example and its smoothed input. From the perspective of the whole iterations for perturbation generation, the proposed loss stabilization term exhaustively searches the perturbation space to smooth the single spot for local optimum escape. We further analyze the KL-divergence of the proposed loss function and find that the loss stabilization term makes the perturbations updated towards a fixed objective spot while deviating from the ground truth. This stabilization ensures the proposed medical attack effective for different types of medical images while producing perturbations in small variance. Experiments on several medical image analysis benchmarks including the recent COVID-19 dataset show the stability of the proposed method.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Computer Aided Diagnosis (CADx) has been widely applied in the medical screening process. The automatic diagnosis benefits doctors to efficiently obtain health status to avoid disease exacerbation. Recently, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have been utilized in CADx to improve the diagnosis accuracy. The discriminative representations improve the performance of medical image analysis including lesion localization, segmentation and disease classification. However, recent advances in adversarial examples have revealed that the deployed CADx systems are usually fragile to adversarial attacks (Finlayson et al., 2019), e.g., small perturbations applied to the input images can deceive CNNs to have opposite conclusions. As mentioned in Ma et al. (2020), the vast amount of money in the healthcare economy may attract attackers to commit insurance fraud or false claims of medical reimbursement by manipulating medical reports. Moreover, image noise is a common issue during the data collection process and sometimes these noise perturbations could implicitly form adversarial attacks. For example, particle contamination of optical lens in dermoscopy and endoscopy and metal/respiratory artifacts of CT scans frequently deteriorate the quality of collected images. Therefore, there is a growing interest to investigate how medical diagnosis systems respond to adversarial attacks and what we can do to improve the robustness of the deployed systems.
While recent studies of adversarial attacks mainly focus on natural images, the research of adversarial attacks in the medical image domain is desired as there are significant differences between

two domains. Beyond regular RGB cameras, there are various types of medical imaging equipments (e.g., Computed Tomography (CT) scanners, ultrasound transducers and fundus cameras) to generate dramatically different images. Fig. 1 shows three examples where an image captured from fundus camera is in (a), an image captured from the CT scanner is in (e) and an endoscopic video frame is in (i). As can be seen in the figure that these three images have little in common. The huge data variance across different modalities of medical images brings more challenges to develop a technology that works for all the modalities. In addition, existing investigations on medical adversarial attacks are limited. In Finlayson et al. (2019), adversarial examples are shown to deteriorate the diagnosis accuracy of deep learning based medical systems. These medical attack methods are mainly based on those from natural images (e.g., Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM) (Goodfellow et al., 2014) and Project Gradient Descent (PGD) (Madry et al., 2017), which are insufficiently developed for different types of medical data. As shown in Fig. 1, the adversarial examples generated by FGSM and PGD do not consistently decrease the network’s performance in (b), (c), (f), (g), (j) and (k). The data variance in (a) and (e) leads to the inconsistent attack results by existing methods.

In this paper, we propose a medical image attack method to consistently produce adversarial perturbations that can fool deep medical diagnosis systems working with different medical data modalities. The perturbations are iteratively generated via taking partial derivatives of a well-defined objective function that is composed of a deviation loss term and a stabilized loss term with respect to the input. By maximizing the deviation loss term, the adversarial attack system enlarges the divergence between CNN predictions and the ground truth to have effective attack samples. To handle the aforementioned ubiquitous data noise issue in medical images, we propose a novel stabilization loss term as an extra regularization, which ensures a consistent deviation trajectory for the crafted attack samples. Meanwhile, the stabilization term avoids the local optima in the optimization process caused by the image noise.

The proposed stabilization loss term is designed to measure the difference between two CNN predictions, where the first prediction is from the crafted adversarial sample and the second one is from the same sample processed with a Gaussian smoothing. Given an adversarial example _A_ and its Gaussian smoothed result _A_[�] , the loss stabilization term constrains the corresponding CNN predictions (i.e., _f_ ( _A_ ) and _f_ ( _A_[�] )) to be similar via a minimization process. The intuition from the scale space optimization (Lindeberg, 1992) indicates that the minimization of _f_ ( _A_ ) and _f_ ( _A_[�] ) will exhaustively search the perturbation space to smooth the single spot for local optimum escape. We further analyze this stabilized loss term via KL-divergence and find that the CNN predictions are steered towards a fixed objective spot during iterations. This stabilization improves the attack effectiveness on different types of medical data including CT, fundus, and endoscopic images. We evaluate the proposed Stablized Medical Image Attack (SMIA) on several medical datasets (APT, 2019; EAD, 2019; Kag, 2015), including the recent COVID-19 (COV, 2019) lung CT. Thorough evaluations demonstrate that the proposed method is effective to produce perturbations that decrease the prediction accuracy of different medical diagnosis systems. Our investigation provides a guidance for strengthening the robustness of these medical systems towards adversarial attacks.

## 2 RELATED WORK

In this section, we literately review existing adversarial attack methods on both natural and medical images. Meanwhile, we survey relevant medical image analysis tasks where SMIA is deployed.

## 2.1 ADVERSARIAL ATTACK

There are extensive investigations on adversarial attacks for natural image classifications. In Goodfellow et al. (2014), FGSM was proposed to generate adversarial examples based on the CNN gradients. A DeepFool method was proposed in Moosavi-Dezfooli et al. (2016) to compute minimal perturbations based on classifier’s linearization. In Moosavi-Dezfooli et al. (2017), an iterative algorithm was proposed to generate perturbations and showed the existence of a universal (imageagnostic) adversarial perturbations. In Baluja & Fischer (2017), a Transformation Network (ATN) was trained to generate adversarial examples without gradient involvement. The adversarial training and provable defense were proposed in Balunovic & Vechev (2020) to achieve both attack robustness and high accuracy. The capsule based reconstructive attack was proposed in Qin et al. (2020) to cause both misclassifications and reconstructive errors. Besides image classification, several attack methods were proposed for semantic segmentation, object detection and object tracking Jia et al. (2020). In Fischer et al. (2017); Dong et al. (2019), the classification based attacks were shown transferable to attack deep image segmentation results. The universal perturbations were demonstrated existing in Moosavi-Dezfooli et al. (2017). Moreover, a Dense Adversary Generation (DAG) method was proposed Xie et al. (2017) for both semantic segmentation and object detection attacks. The general idea of natural image attacks was to iteratively generate perturbations based on the CNN gradients to maximize the network predictions of adversarial examples and the ground truth labels. This idea was also reflected in Finlayson et al. (2019) to show the medical attacks. Different from existing methods, we propose a stabilized regularization term to ensure the consistent generation of adversarial perturbations, which are effective for different types of medical image datasets.

## 2.2 DEEP MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS

The deep Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have been shown effective to automatically analyze medical images (Litjens et al., 2017; Razzak et al., 2018). The common CADx applications include classifying the stage of disease, the detection and segmentation of organs and lesions.

**Disease classification.** Most medical systems formulate disease diagnosis as an image classification task. The types of diseases are predefined and each type corresponds to one category. During the classification process, there are single or multiple images as input for disease diagnosis. In Shen et al. (2015), a multi-scale CNN was proposed to capture the feature representation of lung nodule patches for accurate classification. A multi-instance layer and a multi-scale layer were proposed in Li et al. (2019) to diagnose diabetic macular edema and myopic macular degeneration. Besides diagnosing the types of disease, existing medical systems were also able to predict the disease status (Gulshan et al., 2016) by empirical status categorization.

3

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

**Organ and lesion detection.** The detection of organ and lesion is inspired by the object detection framework for natural images (e.g., Faster-RCNN (Ren et al., 2015), FPN (Lin et al., 2017), and Yolo (Redmon et al., 2016)). Besides, 3D spatial information of the medical data is explored in the 3D detection framework. In Ding et al. (2017), a 3D-CNN classification approach was proposed to classify lung nodule candidates that were previously detected by the Faster-RCNN detector. The RPN (Ren et al., 2015) was extended in Liao et al. (2019) to become 3D-RPN for 3D proposal generation to detect lung nodules. Different from the 3D detection framework, a multi-scale booster was proposed in Shao et al. (2019) with channel and spatial attentions that were integrated into FPN for suspicious lesion detection in 2D CT slices. The detection methods based on 2D image input reduced heavy computational cost brought in the 3D inputs for 3D detection methods.

**Organ and lesion segmentation.** The medical segmentation was significantly advanced via deep encoder-decoder structures (e.g., U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015)). This architecture contains a contracting path (i.e., encoder) to capture global context and a symmetric expanding path (i.e., decoder) to obtain precise localization. There were several works built upon U-Net. In Brosch et al. (2016), skip connections were utilized in the first and last convolutional layers to segment lesions in brain. A V-Net was proposed in Milletari et al. (2016) to segment brain’s anatomical structures. It followed 3D U-Net structure consisting of 3D convolutional layers. A dual pathway and multi-scale 3D-CNN architecture was proposed in Kamnitsas et al. (2017) to generate both global and local lesion representations in the CNN for brain lesion segmentation. Existing medical methods mainly utilized deep encoder-decoder architectures for end-to-end segmentation of organs and lesions.

As illustrated above, deep medical diagnosis systems differ much from the CNN architectures that are developed for natural images. Moreover, the variance of different modalities of medical images is significantly larger than that of natural images. Therefore, the adversarial attacks designed for natural images are not often effective in the medical domain. Nevertheless, the limitations that are brought by huge network and data variance are effectively solved via our stabilized medical attack.

## 3 PROPOSED METHOD

In this section, we illustrate the details of medical image attacks. We first show the objective function of SMIA that consists of a loss deviation term and a loss stabilization term. The loss deviation term produces the perturbation to decrease the image analysis performance, while the loss stabilization term is consistently updated during iterations and constrains these perturbations to low variance. Then, we analyze how SMIA affects the generated perturbations during iterative optimization from the perspective of KL-divergence. The analysis is followed by a visualization showing the variance and cosine distance of perturbations by utilizing the loss stabilization term.

## 3.3 VISUALIZATIONS

The analysis on _L_ STA from KL-divergence indicates that the iteratively generated perturbations are in small variance and updated in a more consistent direction. We visualize the perturbations in different iterations in Fig. 2 to see how perturbations vary in practice. We send an input fundus image to the ResNet-50 (He et al., 2016) integrated with a graph convolutional network (Kipf & Welling, 2016) framework for image classification. The perturbations are generated via Eq. 1 and Eq. 4 independently.

Fig. 2 shows the visual comparison of perturbations generated on the first, fourth, and ninth iterations. We denote the perturbations generated via _L_ DEV (i.e., Eq. 1) as _P_ DEV and that generated via _L_ DEV + _L_ STA (i.e., Eq. 4) as _P_ DEV+STA. The visualization indicates that at the same iteration, the variance of _P_ DEV is larger than that of _P_ DEV+STA. Meanwhile, during different iterations, there are severe variance oscillations in _P_ DEV while _P_ DEV+STA is consistently decreased. On the other side, we calculate the cosine distance between _P_ DEV and _P_ DEV+STA separately. The cosine distance indicates that _P_ DEV is updated in diverse directions while _P_ DEV+STA is more consistent. The visualization accords with the KL-divergence analysis that the proposed loss stabilization term decreases the perturbation variance and constrain a stable update direction towards a fixed objective spot in the CNN feature space.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

We evaluate the proposed method on three medical image analysis tasks including diabetic retinopathy grading, artefact detection, and lung segmentation. The diabetic retinopathy grading is to classify fundus images into predefined categories for diabetes status estimation. The artefact detection is to detect specific artefacts like pixel saturations, motion blur, and specular reflections in the endoscopic images. Lung segmentation is to segment lung region from the whole CT slice. The medical data in one task is significantly different from that in others.

We use two datasets for diabetic retinopathy grading. One is the APTOS-2019 (APT, 2019) dataset with 3,662 fundus images. The other is a large-scale Kaggle-DR (Kag, 2015) dataset where we randomly select 11,000 fundus images from its original training set. Both APTOS-2019 and KaggleDR contains five defined categories. For artefact detection we use EAD-2019 (EAD, 2019) dataset with 2,500 images collected from endoscopic video frames and annotated artefact regions with seven defined categories. These detection images focus on multiple image modalities (i.e., gastroscopy, cystoscopy, gastro-oesophageal and colonoscopy), and are captured in multi-resolution with multimodal (i.e., white light, fluorescence, and narrow band imaging). For lung segmentation, we use the COVID-19 dataset (COV, 2019) where there are 20 CT scans for lungs infected by COVID-19.

In practice, we use ResNet-50 (He et al., 2016) integrated with graph convolutional network (Kipf & Welling, 2016) to classify fundus images, the multi-scale booster framework (Shao et al., 2019) to detect artefact, and U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015) to segment infected lung regions. The proposed method is compared to other adversarial attack methods including FGSM (Goodfellow et al., 2014), PGD (Madry et al., 2017), DeepFool (Moosavi-Dezfooli et al., 2016), and DAG (Xie et al., 2017). The comparisons are made by adding these generated perturbations on the input images for decreased performance observation. For evaluation metrics, we use mean accuracy for medical image classification, IoU and mean Average Precision (mAP) for detection, and mean accuracy and Jaccard index for segmentation. During perturbation generation, we set _ϵ_ as 0 _._ 05, 0 _._ 01, 5 _×_ 10 _[−]_[5] for lung segmentation, artefact detection and diabetic retinopathy grading respectively. We stop at the 10-th iteration for all the attack methods. The _α_ in Eq. 4 is set as 1. We provide more results including using different Gaussian kernels _W_ and show the pseudo code in the supplementary files. Our implementation will be made available to the public.

Table 1 shows the ablation study results on three datasets. We denote “Clean” as the results generated by medical diagnosis systems without adding perturbations. These results indicate the upper limit of these systems. In general, the perturbations generated via deviation loss term (DEV) decrease the recognition performance (i.e., segmentation, detection, and classification results in COVID-19, EAD-2019, and ATPOS-2019, respectively). By integrating loss stabilization term (STA), we are able to generate perturbations that decrease the recognition performance even further. On the other hand, the percentage of positive cosine distance values of DEV+STA is higher than DEV, which indicates the perturbations generated via DEV+STA are more consistent with each other during their iterative generation process. The results shown in this table indicate that STA improves the adversarial attack with DEV alone on both performance deterioration and perturbation consistency on three medical analysis scenarios.

Next, we analyze how the hyper-parameters of existing attack methods affect the attack performance on the EAD-2019 dataset. Tables 2-4 show the analysis results. We evaluate attack performance by using different iteration numbers in Table 2, different values of _ϵ_ in Table 3, and different values of _α_ in Table 4, respectively. The analysis on iteration numbers indicates that existing attack methods decrease the artefact detection performance by increasing the iteration numbers. SMIA significantly decreases the performance by using a limited number of iterations. The analysis on _ϵ_ shows that SMIA performs favorably against existing methods when it is set as 0.01. Besides, SMIA decreases most when _α_ = 1. The hyper-parameter analysis shows our optimal parameter selections.

## 4.2 COMPARISONS WITH STATE-OF-THE-ART

We compare the proposed SMIA method with existing adversarial attack methods in the same medical analysis scenarios. Besides the prevalent FGSM and PGD methods, we involve DAG which is

proposed for detection and segmentation of natural images, and DeepFool that is for disease image classification. All these methods are deployed on the medical diagnosis systems to show the recognition performance decrease. Meanwhile, we compute the variance of all the generated perturbations, which is shown as “Var” in the results.

Table 6 shows the evaluation performance. Under lung segmentation (i.e., COVID-19) and diabetic retinopathy grading (i.e., APTOS-2019 and Kaggle-DR) scenarios, our SMIA consistently decreases the recognition performance of medical systems. The drops in accuracy and Jaccard index brought by our method are more than those from existing methods. Specifically, the accuracy drop on the COVID-19 dataset brought by our method is around 9% more than the second best method (i.e., DAG). Moreover, the accuracy drop by using our method on the APTOS-19 dataset is around 10% more than the second best method (i.e., PGD). The false positive rate brought by our method is higher than other methods in Kaggle-DR. These performance drops indicate the effectiveness of our method to handle medical image data. Under the artefact detection scenario in EAD-2019, we observe that the decrease of mAP from DAG is more than ours. This is because DAG synthesizes pseudo labels for each proposal from the detection network to specifically attack the classifier of the detector. Nevertheless, its performance decrease is not as significant as ours (i.e., 7.62% v.s. 4.8%) under the IoU metric that measures the bounding box overlap ratios. On the other hand, the perturbation variance (i.e., “Var”) of our method is lower than other attack methods on all the evaluation datasets. The decreased performance and smaller perturbations indicate the effectiveness of our SMIA method for attacking medical diagnosis systems.

Besides medical image datasets, we evaluate SMIA on a hand-written image dataset MNIST (Deng, 2012) with existing methods. Table 5 shows the results. Our SMIA method decreases the recognition accuracy more than that of FGSM and PGD. This indicates that SMIA is effective to attack not only medical images, but also images from other fields.

## 5 CONCLUSION

We proposed a medical image adversarial attack method to diagnose current medical systems. The proposed method iteratively generates adversarial perturbations by maximizing the deviation loss term and minimizing the loss stabilization term. For an adversarial example at the current iteration, the difference between its CNN prediction and the corresponding ground truth label is enlarged, while the CNN predictions of its smoothed input and itself are similar. The similar predictions constrain the perturbations to be generated in a relatively flat region in the CNN feature space. During consecutive iterations, the perturbations are updated from one flat region to another. Thus the proposed method can search the perturbation space to smooth the single spot for local optimum escape. Compared to the perturbation movement among single spots that are brought by only using loss deviation term, the loss stabilization term improves the attack performance by regularizing the perturbation movement to be stable. Further analysis with the KL-divergence shows that the minimization of loss stabilization term is to regularize the perturbations to move towards a fixed objective spot while their variances are reduced. Both the visualization and experimental results have shown the effectiveness of our attack method to figure out the limitations of medical diagnosis systems for further improvement.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a 'Stabilized Medical Image Attack' (SMIA) method, which adds a loss stabilization term to standard adversarial perturbation generation to improve consistency across different medical imaging modalities. The authors argue that this term helps escape local optima caused by image noise and ensures that perturbations update in a more stable direction, as supported by a KL-divergence analysis.

The paper addresses a valid and important concern regarding the fragility of medical diagnostic systems to adversarial noise. The proposed stabilization mechanism is intuitive, and the empirical results demonstrate consistent performance gains across diverse medical imaging tasks. However, the lack of clinical validation and the reliance on standard computer vision metrics leave the practical significance of the findings somewhat ambiguous.

Overview issues:

- Lack of clinical relevance in perturbation constraints: The paper uses standard L-infinity based perturbation constraints without justifying their clinical relevance. In medical imaging, the 'imperceptibility' of a perturbation is not merely a matter of pixel-wise distance, but whether the noise alters diagnostic features or mimics pathology. The authors should incorporate structural similarity metrics (like SSIM) or consult expert radiologists to validate that these adversarial examples are truly indistinguishable from clinical artifacts or noise distributions found in real-world acquisition systems.

- Unjustified assumption regarding Gaussian smoothing: In Section 3.1, the authors define the stabilization term using Gaussian smoothing (Eq. 3) to handle image noise. However, they do not justify why Gaussian smoothing is the appropriate model for the complex, non-Gaussian noise distributions inherent in CT or endoscopic images. If the noise model in the data differs significantly from the Gaussian kernel used in the loss function, the stabilization term might be optimizing for an irrelevant feature space. The authors need to demonstrate that this smoothing approach is effective against the specific noise patterns (e.g., metal artifacts, motion blur) mentioned in the introduction.

- Inadequate evaluation of domain-specific preprocessing: Medical images undergo specific preprocessing steps, such as windowing (for CT) and intensity normalization, which are not explicitly accounted for in the attack methodology. If the attack is generated on the raw data but the model operates on windowed/normalized data, the efficacy of the perturbation may be severely diminished. The paper fails to clarify whether the adversarial perturbations are applied before or after these critical clinical preprocessing steps, which is necessary to assess the real-world threat model.

- Correlation between attack metrics and diagnostic error: The authors rely on standard metrics like accuracy, IOU, and mAP to measure attack success. While these are common in computer vision, they do not necessarily correlate with clinical diagnostic error rates. A small drop in mAP might be clinically insignificant, or conversely, a minor perturbation could lead to a catastrophic misdiagnosis of a critical pathology. The paper would be significantly strengthened by mapping these performance drops to clinical outcomes or by providing a qualitative analysis of how the adversarial examples impact diagnostic decisions.

- Lack of empirical validation against clinically relevant noise models: The stabilization loss term relies on Gaussian smoothing to mitigate local optima, yet the paper fails to demonstrate that this specific kernel aligns with the actual noise distributions found in the clinical datasets used (e.g., CT metal artifacts or endoscopic motion blur). To substantiate the claim that SMIA handles 'ubiquitous data noise,' the authors should compute the attack success rate when the stabilization kernel is matched against the specific noise characteristics of the COVID-19 CT and EAD-2019 datasets. A simulation showing that SMIA maintains performance in the presence of non-Gaussian, modality-specific noise—rather than just Gaussian-smoothed inputs—is necessary to prove the method is not merely overfitting to a specific smoothing operator.

- Absence of sensitivity analysis regarding clinical preprocessing pipelines: The paper treats the medical imaging pipeline as a black box, ignoring standard clinical preprocessing such as CT windowing or intensity normalization. If the adversarial perturbation is added before these non-linear transformations, its magnitude and structure may be drastically altered, potentially neutralizing the attack. The authors need to provide a worked example or a controlled experiment where the attack is generated under a specified preprocessing pipeline (e.g., Hounsfield unit windowing for CT) to verify that the perturbation remains effective post-transformation. Without this, the claim that SMIA is a robust tool for medical diagnosis systems remains speculative regarding its real-world applicability.

- Missing validation of diagnostic impact beyond aggregate performance metrics: The paper relies exclusively on aggregate metrics like mAP and Jaccard index to quantify attack success. In a clinical context, a small shift in these metrics might not reflect the severity of a diagnostic error, such as missing a malignant lesion or misclassifying a critical condition. To demonstrate the 'bite' of the results, the authors should provide a qualitative analysis or a small-scale expert review of specific adversarial examples where the perturbation causes a clinically significant change in diagnostic output. Comparing the model's prediction on a clean versus adversarial image alongside a radiologist's assessment would clarify whether the observed performance drops correspond to genuine diagnostic failures.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper presents a technically sound method for generating adversarial examples, but it fails to bridge the gap between computer vision benchmarks and clinical reality. A revision must address how these perturbations interact with standard medical image processing pipelines and provide a more robust justification for the chosen noise-modeling assumptions.

Revision targets:

- Include a sensitivity analysis showing how the attack performs when subjected to standard clinical preprocessing (e.g., windowing, contrast adjustment).

- Provide a qualitative validation from a domain expert (radiologist) to confirm that the perturbations are not clinically detectable and do not resemble common diagnostic artifacts.

- Justify the choice of Gaussian smoothing as a proxy for medical image noise, or test the method against more realistic noise models specific to the modalities used (CT, fundus, endoscopy).

- Clarify the threat model by specifying whether the attacker has access to the pre- or post-processed images used by the diagnostic system.

comments.title: Contradictory description of PGD performance

comments.explanation: The text claims PGD 'works' in (g), yet the preceding paragraph lists PGD as one of the methods that 'do not consistently decrease the network's performance' in (g). This contradiction regarding the efficacy of PGD on the CT dataset needs to be resolved to clarify the baseline performance.

comments.title: Ambiguity in metric terminology
comments.quote: On the other side, we calculate the cosine distance between  $P_{\mathrm{DEV}}$  and  $P_{\mathrm{DEV} + \mathrm{STA}}$  separately. The cosine distance indicates that  $P_{\mathrm{DEV}}$  is updated in diverse directions while  $P_{\mathrm{DEV} + \mathrm{STA}}$  is more consistent.
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'cosine distance' while Table 1 uses the label 'Cos(%)↑'. Mathematically, cosine distance is minimized when vectors are aligned, meaning a higher value would indicate greater divergence, which contradicts your interpretation of 'consistency.' Please define the metric clearly and ensure the terminology and table labels are consistent.

comments.quote: we set  $\epsilon$  as  $0.05$ ,  $0.01$ ,  $5 \times 10^{-5}$  for lung segmentation, artefact detection and diabetic retinopathy grading respectively.
comments.explanation: The epsilon values vary by three orders of magnitude without justification. Applying a perturbation of 0.05 to CT data is a significant change, while 5 * 10^-5 is negligible. Please clarify the normalization range for each dataset to demonstrate that the attack intensity is actually comparable across these different modalities.

comments.title: Miscalculation of performance drop

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim regarding local optimum escape

comments.explanation: The claim that the method facilitates 'local optimum escape' is an assertion about optimization dynamics that lacks formal support. The provided analysis focuses on variance reduction and stability, not the loss landscape geometry. Please rephrase this to describe the method as improving search stability rather than claiming it provides a mechanism for escaping local optima.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes SMIA, a stabilized adversarial attack for medical images that combines a deviation loss with a stabilization loss derived from KL-divergence analysis. While the cross-modal evaluation shows promise, the manuscript contains a fundamental mathematical inversion in its theoretical derivation, lacks statistical rigor in experiments, and makes unsupported claims about clinical diagnostic impact. Addressing these gaps is necessary before the work can meet top-venue standards.

The paper tackles a relevant problem by attempting to unify adversarial attack generation across diverse medical imaging modalities. The empirical results demonstrate consistent accuracy degradation on CT, fundus, and endoscopic datasets, and the ablation study isolates the contribution of the stabilization term. However, the theoretical justification contains a critical optimization error, and the experimental design omits standard statistical reporting and clinical validation steps that are essential for medical AI research.

Overview issues:

- Mathematical inversion in KL-divergence optimization analysis: Section 3.2 and Equation 8 claim that minimizing the term `Σ 1/p_i` subject to `Σ p_i = 1` yields the uniform distribution `p_i = 1/K`. This is mathematically incorrect; the function is strictly convex over the probability simplex, meaning the uniform distribution is the maximum, not the minimum. The minimum actually occurs at the boundary where one class probability approaches 1. This reversal undermines the theoretical foundation of the stabilization term. If the term genuinely pushes predictions toward uniformity, it may conflict with standard adversarial objectives that require high-confidence misclassification. The authors must correct the optimization derivation, verify the actual behavior of the regularization term, and adjust the theoretical claims to match the corrected analysis.

- Undefined clinical stability and unverified diagnostic impact: The manuscript equates perturbation variance and cosine distance with 'stability,' yet never quantifies how these proxies relate to diagnostic consistency or anatomical plausibility. Pixel-level adversarial perturbations are assumed to cause clinical misdiagnosis without any radiologist evaluation or clinical performance metrics. This gap matters because medical AI safety requires evidence that perturbations do not introduce subtle artifacts that degrade diagnostic reasoning without triggering obvious model failures. The authors should either incorporate a clinical validation step with expert readers or explicitly limit claims to model accuracy degradation and acknowledge the lack of diagnostic relevance testing.

- Absence of statistical significance and experimental rigor: All reported metrics in Tables 1 through 6 lack standard deviations, confidence intervals, or results averaged across multiple random seeds. Medical imaging benchmarks demand statistical testing to distinguish genuine methodological improvements from dataset-specific variance or architectural quirks. Without this, the claimed consistency across CT, fundus, and endoscopic modalities remains unverified. The revision must include standard error bars, report results over at least five independent seeds, and provide a clear dataset split protocol to ensure reproducibility and meet community standards.

- Unrealistic evaluation assumptions and missing domain-specific baselines: The experiments test perturbations on idealized, preprocessed test sets without accounting for real-world medical imaging pipelines like DICOM compression, scanner noise, or reconstruction artifacts. Furthermore, the comparison omits recent medical-specific adversarial attacks and robustness baselines. This matters because adversarial vulnerabilities in medical systems often depend heavily on preprocessing, acquisition conditions, and domain-specific feature distributions. The authors should either evaluate perturbations under realistic degradation conditions or explicitly frame the work as a theoretical model-robustness study, and add comparisons to domain-specific attack methods to contextualize the results.

- Contradiction between theoretical prediction and empirical attack behavior: The KL-divergence analysis predicts that the stabilization term drives CNN predictions toward a uniform distribution `1/K`, yet the experimental results show successful, high-confidence misclassifications (e.g., accuracy drops to ~60% on COVID-19). If predictions were truly uniform, the attack success mechanism would differ fundamentally from what is observed. This discrepancy matters because it suggests the theoretical interpretation does not capture the actual optimization dynamics during perturbation generation. The authors need to reconcile this by visualizing actual prediction distributions during training or clarifying that the KL term only constrains perturbation smoothness rather than forcing uniform outputs.

- Worked special case for the KL-divergence interpretation: Section 3.2 derives a closed-form approximation for the stabilization term via KL-divergence, claiming it pushes predictions toward a uniform distribution. Readers cannot easily verify this claim or understand the gradient modification without a concrete worked example. Computing the exact gradient update for a simple 2-class linear classifier or a single-layer softmax network would clarify how the stabilization term alters the perturbation direction compared to standard PGD. This calibration step is standard for papers making specific claims about optimization dynamics and would help reconcile the theoretical derivation with the empirical results.

- Underdeveloped implications for model robustness: The introduction and conclusion promise that the investigation provides guidance for strengthening the robustness of medical systems. The paper, however, stops at generating attacks. It never demonstrates how to operationalize this guidance, such as by using SMIA perturbations in an adversarial training loop or by defining a stability-aware regularization term for defense. Developing at least one concrete downstream application, like training a robust classifier on SMIA-augmented data and reporting the resulting accuracy drop, would directly deliver on the stated implications and bridge the gap between attack generation and system hardening.

- Missing demonstration of the local optimum escape mechanism: The paper claims the stabilization term enables the optimization to escape local optima by enforcing consistent perturbation directions across iterations. The current ablation study only compares final accuracy and perturbation variance; it never isolates the escape mechanism. A natural next step is to plot the optimization trajectory or loss landscape for a specific image where DEV alone stalls. Showing a case where DEV converges prematurely while DEV+STA continues to improve attack success would validate the core theoretical claim and prove the method has bite beyond marginal accuracy gains.

- Missing practical guidance on hyperparameter scaling across modalities: Practitioners will struggle to apply the method without a discussion on how to scale the Gaussian smoothing kernel W and balancing weight α across different imaging pipelines. The experiments apply a single kernel size and α=1 across CT scans, fundus images, and endoscopic videos, which differ drastically in resolution, pixel intensity ranges, and noise profiles. The paper does not explain how to adjust W relative to image dimensions or modality-specific noise levels, nor does it provide a sensitivity analysis for α across datasets. Adding a practical discussion or a small ablation on kernel scaling would be necessary for external researchers to adapt the method without extensive trial-and-error.

Recommendation: Major revision. The mathematical error in the core theoretical derivation, combined with the lack of statistical rigor and clinical validation, prevents the paper from meeting top-venue standards. Correcting the optimization analysis and adding proper experimental controls are prerequisites for publication.

Revision targets:

- Fix the optimization analysis in Section 3.2 and align theoretical claims with empirical prediction distributions.

- Add standard deviations, multi-seed averaging, and statistical significance testing to all experimental tables.

- Incorporate a clinical plausibility assessment or explicitly limit claims to model accuracy degradation.

- Evaluate perturbations under realistic imaging degradations (compression, noise) and compare against recent medical-specific attack baselines.

comments.title: Rank deficiency in the transformed Fisher Information Matrix
comments.quote: size. We follow *Zhao et al. (2019)* to formulate $\mathbf{G}_{\tilde{x}}$ as a new matrix $\mathbf{G}_{f}$ through $\mathbf{G}_{\tilde{x}}=\mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{G}_{f}\mathbf{J}$. The ter
comments.explanation: This transformation maps a high-dimensional image-space matrix to a $K \times K$ class-output matrix. Since the Jacobian $\mathbf{J}$ has rank at most $K$, the resulting $\mathbf{G}_{\tilde{x}}$ is rank-deficient with at least $D-K$ zero eigenvalues, where $D$ is the image dimension. The paper claims the optimization "seeks the smallest eigenvalue of $\mathbf{G}_{\tilde{x}}$", but the smallest eigenvalues are trivially zero regardless of the perturbation. The derivation needs to clarify that the optimization only affects the non-zero eigenvalues in the class-output subspace, otherwise the mathematical claim about eigenvalue minimization is ill-posed.

comments.title: Optimization direction and sign ambiguity in the stabilization term
comments.quote:
Eq. 2. The loss stabilization term can be written as:

$\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{STA}}=-\mathcal{L}(f(\theta,x+\eta),f(\theta,x+W*\eta)),$ (3)
comments.explanation: The negative sign in Eq. 3 conflicts with the stated goal of enforcing similarity between predictions unless the overall objective is explicitly maximized. Standard loss functions are minimized, so a negative sign would push the predictions apart. The text later states the objective is maximized, but this crucial detail is missing right after Eq. 3. Add a clear statement specifying that the attack maximizes $\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{SMIA}}$ to resolve the sign confusion and align the formulation with adversarial optimization conventions.

comments.title: Gradient flow through the smoothed input is unspecified
comments.quote:
Eq. 2. The loss stabilization term can be written as:

$\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{STA}}=-\mathcal{L}(f(\theta,x+\eta),f(\theta,x+W*\eta)),$ (3)
comments.explanation: When computing gradients to update the perturbation $\eta$, it is unclear whether the optimizer backpropagates through the second argument $f(\theta,x+W*\eta)$. In stability regularization, the smoothed input is typically treated as a fixed target, requiring a gradient stop (e.g., `detach()`). If gradients flow through both arguments, the update direction depends on the specific loss form and may counteract the intended stabilization. Specify whether the second term is detached to clarify the actual gradient computation.

comments.title: Empirical results contradict the claimed optimal $\epsilon$
comments.quote: The analysis on  $\epsilon$  shows that SMIA performs favorably against existing methods when it is set as 0.01.
comments.explanation: Table 3 demonstrates that SMIA achieves lower IoU (1.18%) and mAP (3.25%) at $\epsilon = 0.015$ compared to $\epsilon = 0.01$ (4.80% and 3.47%). Since lower scores indicate stronger attack performance, the empirical data shows continued improvement at the larger perturbation budget. The claim that 0.01 is optimal is unsupported by the table. Clarify whether 0.01 was chosen for a visibility trade-off or correct the statement to match the data.

comments.title: Conflation of absolute IoU scores with performance decreases
comments.quote: or. Nevertheless, its performance decrease is not as significant as ours (i.e.,  $7.62\%$  v.s.  $4.8\%$ ) under the IoU metric that m
comments.explanation: The values 7.62% and 4.8% are absolute IoU scores, not performance drops. Calculating the actual decrease from the clean baseline (22.86%) yields drops of 15.24% for DAG and 18.06% for SMIA. The text incorrectly compares absolute scores as if they were deltas, leading to a false conclusion about relative performance. Rewrite the comparison to explicitly state whether absolute values or deltas are being discussed, and correct the numerical interpretation.

comments.title: Unsupported logical leap regarding attack transferability
comments.quote: Moreover, the variance of different modalities of medical images is significantly larger than that of natural images. Therefore, the adversarial attacks designed for natural images are not often effective in the medical domain.
comments.explanation: The conclusion that natural-image attacks fail on medical data does not follow from the premise of higher modality variance. Transferability across domains is well-documented, and many studies show that standard attacks degrade medical classifiers. Stating this as a settled fact without citation creates a false dichotomy. Rephrase the motivation to frame this as a hypothesis to be tested rather than an established limitation of existing methods.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of the regularization domain
comments.quote: The similar predictions constrain the perturbations to be generated in a relatively flat region in the CNN feature space.
comments.explanation: The stabilization mechanism explicitly applies Gaussian smoothing to the input image and minimizes prediction differences between the original and smoothed inputs. This operates in the input (pixel) space to encourage smoothness of the loss landscape with respect to input perturbations, not in the internal CNN feature space. The conclusion incorrectly locates the regularization domain. Replace "CNN feature space" with "input space" to accurately reflect the method's operation.

comments.title: Contradictory description of the attack objective
comments.quote: The general idea of natural image attacks was to iteratively generate perturbations based on the CNN gradients to maximize the network predictions of adversarial examples and the ground truth labels.
comments.explanation: This sentence misrepresents the core optimization goal. Adversarial attacks aim to maximize the loss or the target class probability while minimizing the probability assigned to the ground truth. Maximizing predictions for both the adversarial output and the ground truth is mathematically impossible and contradicts the definition of an adversarial example. Rewrite this passage to accurately describe the trade-off between target class probability and ground truth probability.

comments.title: Overclaiming exhaustive search capabilities
comments.quote: From the perspective of the whole iterations for perturbation generation, the proposed loss stabilization term exhaustively searches the perturbation space to smooth the single spot for local optimum escape.
comments.explanation: Iterative gradient-based attacks perform local optimization steps by following computed gradients, not an exhaustive search of the entire perturbation space. The stabilization term modifies the gradient trajectory to help escape shallow local minima, but it does not enable a global exhaustive search. Adjust the terminology to reflect the actual optimization dynamics, such as "explores the perturbation landscape" or "regularizes the gradient trajectory".

comments.title: Missing perturbation norm and normalization specification
comments.quote: perturbation generation, we set  $\epsilon$  as  $0.05$ ,  $0.01$ ,  $5 \times 10^{-5}$  for lung segmentation, artefact detection and diabetic retinopathy grading respectively.
comments.explanation: The perturbation budget $\epsilon$ is listed without specifying the $L_p$ norm (typically $L_\infty$ for these attacks) or the pixel value normalization range (e.g., $[0,1]$ or $[-1,1]$). Without this information, the perturbation magnitudes cannot be reproduced or fairly compared to the baselines. Explicitly state the norm and normalization convention to ensure reproducibility.

comments.title: KL-divergence conclusion not supported by formal derivation
comments.quote: Further analysis with the KL-divergence shows that the minimization of loss stabilization term is to regularize the perturbations to move towards a fixed objective spot while their variances are reduced.
comments.explanation: The conclusion asserts a specific outcome of the KL-divergence analysis regarding a "fixed objective spot," but the formal results section only mentions analyzing the loss from the perspective of KL-divergence without deriving this bound or result. The gap between the method description and the claimed analytical outcome needs to be bridged. Either provide the explicit mathematical derivation in the formal section or tone down the conclusion to match the presented analysis.

comments.title: Local optimum escape claim lacks formal backing
comments.quote: During consecutive iterations, the perturbations are updated from one flat region to another. Thus the proposed method can search the perturbation space to smooth the single spot for local optimum escape.
comments.explanation: The conclusion states that the method escapes local optima by smoothing spots in the perturbation space. The formal results section describes the objective function but provides no theoretical analysis of optimization dynamics, convergence properties, or local minima avoidance. Without a formal result or cited theorem regarding the landscape, this claim remains an unverified assertion. Remove the local optimum escape claim or support it with formal optimization analysis.

comments.title: Category error conflating KL-divergence with perturbation direction
comments.quote: We further analyze the KL-divergence of the proposed loss function and find that the loss stabilization term makes the perturbations updated towards a fixed objective spot while deviating from the ground truth.
comments.explanation: KL-divergence quantifies the difference between two probability distributions, typically the CNN's output predictions for the adversarial example and its smoothed input. It does not operate in the spatial or feature space where perturbations reside. Describing the KL-divergence analysis as revealing a "fixed objective spot" for perturbation updates is a category error. The gradient of the KL-divergence term influences the perturbation update direction in the input space, but the divergence metric itself lives in probability space. Rewrite this claim to distinguish between the loss landscape and the perturbation trajectory.

comments.title: Ambiguous reuse of $\mathcal{L}$ for loss and distance metrics
comments.quote:
$\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{SMIA}}$ $=\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{DEV}}+\alpha\cdot\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{STA}}$ (4)
$=\mathcal{L}(f(\theta,x+\eta),Y)-\alpha\cdot\mathcal{L}(f(\theta,x+\eta),f(\theta,x+W*\eta))$
comments.explanation: The symbol $\mathcal{L}$ denotes two fundamentally different quantities: a standard classification loss against ground truth in the first term, and a distance or similarity metric between two model predictions in the second term. This overloading creates ambiguity, particularly regarding gradient computation and the sign analysis in Eq. 3. Use distinct notation, such as $\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{CE}}$ for cross-entropy and $\mathcal{D}$ for the stability distance, to clarify the mathematical structure.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a stabilized adversarial attack method for medical images, combining a deviation loss with a stabilization loss that enforces prediction similarity between an adversarial example and its Gaussian-smoothed version. The core idea is interesting and the paper provides a KL-divergence analysis to motivate the stabilization term. However, the paper has several significant issues: a likely error in the trace computation of the Fisher Information Matrix, an unsupported claim that the stabilization term forces predictions toward a uniform distribution, insufficient experimental detail (e.g., no confidence intervals, no statistical tests), and a lack of comparison to relevant baselines like Gaussian noise augmentation. The contribution is clearly articulated but the technical correctness and empirical rigor need substantial improvement.

Overview issues:

- Trace computation of the Fisher Information Matrix appears incorrect: In Section 3.2, Equation 7, the paper computes the trace of the Fisher Information Matrix G_f as sum_{i=1}^K 1/p_i. This derivation is not standard and appears to contain an error. For a categorical distribution with probabilities p_i, the Fisher information matrix for the log-likelihood with respect to the softmax outputs has entries that depend on the parameterization. The paper's simplification to sum 1/p_i is not generally correct and would need a careful derivation with explicit assumptions about the parameterization of the network outputs. This error propagates to Equation 8, where the stabilization term is claimed to be proportional to (sum 1/p_i) * (eta')^T * eta'. The authors should re-derive this step carefully or provide a citation that justifies this simplification. If the derivation is indeed flawed, the theoretical motivation for the stabilization term is weakened.

- Claim that stabilization forces predictions toward uniform distribution is unsupported: The paper argues that minimizing sum 1/p_i subject to sum p_i = 1 leads to p_i = 1/K for all i, implying the stabilization term drives CNN predictions toward a uniform distribution. However, this conclusion depends on the flawed trace derivation. Moreover, even if the trace were correctly derived, the stabilization term is only one component of the overall objective; the deviation loss simultaneously pushes predictions away from the ground truth. The paper does not analyze the combined effect. The claim that perturbations move 'towards a fixed objective spot' that is 'not related to network structure nor data type' is therefore not substantiated. The authors should either provide a rigorous analysis of the combined objective or temper these claims.

- No statistical significance or variability reported for experimental results: All experimental results in Tables 1-6 are reported as single numbers without any measure of variability (e.g., standard deviation, confidence intervals) or statistical significance tests. Given that the paper claims SMIA outperforms baselines, it is essential to show that these differences are not due to random chance. This is particularly important because the perturbation generation process involves randomness (e.g., initialization, Gaussian smoothing). The authors should repeat experiments multiple times with different random seeds and report means and standard deviations, or at least provide error bars for key comparisons.

- Missing comparison to simple baselines like Gaussian noise augmentation: The paper compares SMIA to FGSM, PGD, DeepFool, and DAG, but does not compare to a simple baseline: adding Gaussian noise to the input. Since the stabilization term involves Gaussian smoothing, it is natural to ask whether simply adding Gaussian noise (without any gradient-based optimization) could achieve similar attack performance. Such a baseline would help isolate the contribution of the gradient-based optimization from the smoothing operation. The authors should include a 'Gaussian noise' baseline in the comparisons.

- Stabilization term uses the same loss function as deviation term without justification: In Equation 3, the stabilization loss L_STA is defined as -L(f(theta, x+eta), f(theta, x+W*eta)), using the same loss function L (cross-entropy) as the deviation term. However, the stabilization term is meant to enforce similarity between predictions, not to maximize divergence. Using cross-entropy for this purpose is unusual because cross-entropy is not symmetric and is designed for classification, not for measuring similarity between two distributions. The paper should justify why cross-entropy is appropriate here, or consider using a symmetric divergence measure like KL-divergence or Jensen-Shannon divergence.

- The Gaussian kernel W is not specified and its effect is not analyzed: The paper introduces a Gaussian kernel W that convolves with the perturbation eta in Equation 3, but never specifies the kernel size or standard deviation. The choice of W likely affects the stabilization term's behavior, yet no ablation study or sensitivity analysis is provided. The authors should specify the kernel parameters and include an experiment varying them to show robustness.

- No demonstration that SMIA attacks succeed where baselines fail: The paper claims SMIA produces effective attacks across CT, fundus, and endoscopic images, but the experiments in Table 6 show that on EAD-2019 (endoscopic images), DAG achieves lower mAP (2.71%) than SMIA (3.47%). The paper dismisses this by noting DAG uses pseudo-labels, but this undercuts the claim of consistent superiority. More importantly, the paper never shows a concrete example where SMIA succeeds and all baselines fail. The motivating Figure 1 shows FGSM and PGD failing on some modalities, but SMIA is never demonstrated on those exact failure cases. The authors should add a table or figure showing, for each modality, the specific images or cases where SMIA degrades performance while all baselines fail to do so, with statistical significance over multiple runs.

- Missing worked parametric example to illustrate the method: The paper derives a complex objective with a stabilization term and provides a KL-divergence analysis, but never computes the SMIA objective for a simple, fully-specified model. A natural example would be a binary classification problem with logistic regression on a 2D Gaussian mixture. The authors could compute the deviation and stabilization losses explicitly, show how the perturbation evolves over iterations, and verify that the stabilization term indeed reduces variance and steers predictions toward uniformity. Without such an example, readers cannot calibrate intuitions about the method's behavior, and the theoretical claims remain abstract.

- No discussion of how to estimate or implement the method in practice: The paper claims practical relevance for attacking medical diagnosis systems, but provides no guidance on implementation choices that affect reproducibility. The Gaussian kernel W is never specified (size, standard deviation). The choice of α=1 is used without sensitivity analysis on all datasets. The stopping criterion (10 iterations) is fixed without justification. Most critically, the method requires computing predictions on both the adversarial sample and its Gaussian-smoothed version at each iteration, which doubles the forward passes. The paper should include a pseudo-code algorithm, specify all hyperparameters with ranges, and discuss computational cost. Without this, practitioners cannot replicate or deploy SMIA.

- Underdeveloped claim that SMIA works across diverse modalities: The abstract and introduction emphasize that SMIA works across CT, fundus, and endoscopic images, but the experiments only evaluate three specific tasks (segmentation, detection, classification) on four datasets. The claim of 'consistently producing adversarial perturbations on medical images' implies a general method, yet the paper does not test on other common modalities like ultrasound, MRI, or X-ray. The authors should either add experiments on at least one additional modality (e.g., chest X-ray from CheXpert or MIMIC-CXR) or explicitly limit the claim to the tested modalities. As written, the scope of the claim exceeds the evidence.

Recommendation: Major revision. The core idea is interesting and the paper addresses an important problem, but the theoretical derivation contains a likely error, key claims are unsupported, and the experimental evaluation lacks statistical rigor. The paper needs substantial corrections and additions before it can be considered for publication.

Revision targets:

- Correct the derivation of the trace of the Fisher Information Matrix in Section 3.2, or remove the flawed theoretical analysis and rely on empirical motivation.

- Add confidence intervals or standard deviations to all experimental results (Tables 1-6) by repeating experiments multiple times.

- Include a baseline of adding Gaussian noise to the input to isolate the effect of the smoothing operation from gradient-based optimization.

- Justify the use of cross-entropy for the stabilization term or replace it with a symmetric divergence measure.

- Specify the Gaussian kernel parameters (size, sigma) and include an ablation study showing sensitivity to these parameters.

comments.title: Claim of consistent superiority contradicted by EAD-2019 mAP results
comments.quote: e artefact detection scenario in EAD-2019, we observe that the decrease of mAP from DAG is more than ours. This is because DAG synthesizes pseudo labels for each proposal from the detection network to specifically attack the classifier of the detector. Nevertheless, its performance decrease is not as significant as ours (i.e.,  $7.62\%$  v.s.  $4.8\%$ ) under the IoU metric that measures the bounding box overlap ratios.
comments.explanation: The paper's own data in Table 6 shows that on EAD-2019, DAG achieves mAP=2.71% (a drop of 40.65 percentage points from clean 43.36%) while SMIA achieves mAP=3.47% (a drop of 39.89 percentage points). The paper then switches to the IoU metric (7.62% vs 4.8%) to claim superiority, but this is a different metric. The claim that SMIA 'consistently decreases the recognition performance' is not supported on this dataset under the primary metric (mAP). The authors should either acknowledge that DAG outperforms SMIA on mAP for EAD-2019, or provide a principled reason why IoU is the more appropriate metric here. The current text is misleading.

comments.title: Variance comparison uses different scales across methods
comments.quote:
overlap ratios. On the other hand, the perturbation variance (i.e., "Var") of our method is lower than other attack methods on all the evaluation datasets. The decreased performance and smaller perturbations indicate the effectiveness of our SMIA method for attacking medical diagnosis systems.

Besides
comments.explanation: The variance values in Table 6 are reported without any normalization or explanation of how they are computed. For example, on COVID-19, SMIA Var=0.001 while FGSM Var=0.087 — a 87x difference. But on EAD-2019, SMIA Var=0.019 while FGSM Var=0.062 — only a 3x difference. The paper does not specify whether these variances are computed over perturbation pixels, over images, or over some other unit. Without this information, the reader cannot assess whether the claimed 'smaller perturbations' are meaningful. The authors should define exactly what 'Var' measures and report it in a way that allows cross-method comparison (e.g., normalized by perturbation magnitude).

comments.title: MNIST results referenced but not shown in the section
comments.quote: Besides medical image datasets, we evaluate SMIA on a hand-written image dataset MNIST (Deng, 2012) with existing methods. Table 5 shows the results. Our SMIA method decreases the recognition accuracy more than that of FGSM and PGD. This indicates that SMIA is effective to attack not only medical images, but also images from other fields.
comments.explanation: Table 5 is not included in this section. The reader cannot verify the claim that SMIA outperforms FGSM and PGD on MNIST. Moreover, the claim that SMIA is 'effective to attack not only medical images, but also images from other fields' is a strong generalization from a single, simple dataset (MNIST). The authors should either include Table 5 in this section or remove the reference. If the table is in another section, a cross-reference should be provided.

comments.title: DeepFool and DAG results missing for multiple datasets
comments.explanation: DeepFool results are only reported for APTOS-2019 and Kaggle-DR, not for COVID-19 or EAD-2019. DAG results are only reported for COVID-19 and EAD-2019, not for APTOS-2019 or Kaggle-DR. This makes it impossible to compare all methods on all datasets. The paper claims to compare 'with state-of-the-art' but the comparison is incomplete. The authors should either run all methods on all datasets or explain why certain methods cannot be applied (e.g., DAG requires detection networks, which may not exist for APTOS-2019). The current presentation is inconsistent.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a Stabilized Medical Image Attack (SMIA) method that adds a regularization term to standard adversarial attack objectives to improve consistency across medical modalities. While the method is technically sound and demonstrates improved performance and stability compared to standard attacks like FGSM and PGD, the paper suffers from critical gaps in experimental validation and theoretical justification. The most significant issues are the lack of robustness analysis against defensive perturbations (e.g., JPEG compression, denoising), the absence of a comprehensive comparison with recent lesion-focused attacks, and the incomplete analysis of the theoretical claim that the method steers predictions toward a uniform distribution.

The paper presents a novel regularization term for adversarial attacks that shows promise in stabilizing perturbations across different medical modalities. The empirical results are convincing, showing lower variance and higher attack success rates compared to baselines. However, the contribution is not fully realized due to missing analyses that are standard in top-tier venues. Specifically, the method lacks evaluation against common post-processing defenses, a comparison with more recent lesion-targeting attacks, and a deeper theoretical analysis of the KL-divergence claim. The paper is technically competent but incomplete as a research contribution.

Overview issues:

- Missing Defense Robustness Analysis: The paper evaluates SMIA against standard gradient-based attacks (FGSM, PGD) but does not test its robustness against common defensive post-processing techniques used in medical imaging pipelines, such as JPEG compression, denoising (e.g., BM3D), or adversarial training. These defenses are widely deployed in production systems and are standard evaluation criteria for attack papers. Without this analysis, the practical applicability and 'real-world' effectiveness of the proposed method remain questionable. The authors should add experiments where generated adversarial examples are passed through a JPEG compression step (e.g., quality factor 75) or a denoising filter before being fed into the target model. If the perturbations are destroyed by these common operations, the method's utility for realistic attacks is significantly diminished.

- Incomplete Comparison with Recent Lesion-Focused Attacks: The literature review cites recent works like LatAtk (2025) that focus on lesion areas to improve transferability, but the experimental section does not include a comparison with these methods. The paper claims SMIA is effective across modalities, but lesion-focused attacks are specifically designed to overcome the high variance issue in medical images by targeting pathology. Comparing SMIA against these methods would provide a clearer picture of its relative performance and whether the proposed stabilization term offers advantages over lesion-aware strategies. The authors should include LatAtk or similar methods in the comparison tables to demonstrate if SMIA outperforms or complements lesion-focused approaches.

- Theoretical Claim Lacks Rigorous Justification: The paper claims that the stabilization term steers CNN predictions towards a uniform distribution $[1/K, ..., 1/K]$ based on a Taylor expansion of KL-divergence and the minimization of $	ext{tr}(G_f)$. However, this derivation is heuristic and assumes the minimization of the second term in Eq. 8 leads to a uniform distribution, which is a strong claim not fully proven. The Taylor expansion is an approximation, and the minimization of $	ext{tr}(G_f)$ does not necessarily guarantee convergence to a uniform distribution in a non-linear deep network. The authors should provide a more rigorous analysis or acknowledge the limitations of this claim. A more accurate interpretation might be that the method minimizes the Fisher Information Matrix, which relates to the flatness of the decision boundary, rather than explicitly steering towards a uniform distribution.

- Lack of Analysis on Perturbation Transferability: The paper focuses on the consistency and variance of perturbations but does not analyze the transferability of SMIA-generated examples to other models or modalities. A key advantage of adversarial attacks is their ability to transfer to unseen models, which is crucial for evaluating the robustness of a system. The paper's main claim is that SMIA is effective across different medical image modalities, but it does not explicitly measure this transferability. The authors should include experiments where perturbations generated on one dataset (e.g., fundus images) are applied to a different modality (e.g., CT images) or a different model architecture to demonstrate the cross-modal transferability of the method.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add experiments evaluating SMIA's robustness against common defensive post-processing techniques such as JPEG compression and denoising (e.g., BM3D).

- Include a comparison with recent lesion-focused adversarial attack methods (e.g., LatAtk) to contextualize SMIA's performance relative to state-of-the-art medical-specific attacks.

- Provide a more rigorous theoretical analysis or clarification of the claim that the method steers predictions towards a uniform distribution, acknowledging the limitations of the Taylor expansion approximation.

- Include an analysis of perturbation transferability across different medical modalities and model architectures to better support the paper's claim of cross-modality effectiveness.

comments.title: Inconsistent Data Reporting in Table 6
comments.explanation: The table reports 'Var' (variance) for FGSM, PGD, and DeepFool on the Kaggle-DR dataset, but the text claims 'the perturbation variance (i.e., "Var") of our method is lower than other attack methods on all the evaluation datasets.' comments.title: Ambiguous claim about cosine distance
comments.quote: cosine distance indicates that  $P_{\mathrm{DEV}}$  is updated in diverse directions while  $P_{\mathrm{DEV} + \mathrm{STA}}$  is more consistent. The
comments.explanation: The claim that $P_{	ext{DEV}}$ is updated in diverse directions while $P_{	ext{DEV} + 	ext{STA}}$ is more consistent is ambiguous. The cosine distance measures the similarity between two vectors, not the direction of update. A high cosine distance between two perturbation vectors indicates that they are dissimilar, which could mean that the perturbations are changing significantly between iterations (diverse directions) or that the perturbations are not converging to a single direction (inconsistent). The authors should clarify what they mean by 'diverse directions' and 'consistent' in the context of cosine distance. For example, they could state that the cosine distance between perturbations at different iterations is high for $P_{	ext{DEV}}$ and low for $P_{	ext{DEV} + 	ext{STA}}$, indicating that the perturbations are changing significantly for DEV and converging for DEV + STA.

comments.title: Missing explanation for STA's effect on variance
comments.quote: The visualization indicates that at the same iteration, the variance of  $P_{\mathrm{DEV}}$  is larger than that of  $P_{\mathrm{DEV} + \mathrm{STA}}$ .
comments.explanation: The text states that the visualization indicates that the variance of $P_{	ext{DEV}}$ is larger than that of $P_{	ext{DEV} + 	ext{STA}}$, but it does not explain why this is the case. The authors should provide a brief explanation for why the STA term reduces the variance of the perturbations. For example, they could state that the STA term constrains the perturbations to a smaller region of the perturbation space, which reduces the variance. This would help readers understand the mechanism behind the STA term and its effect on the perturbation generation process.
